coredata.dc:title: A review on possible mechanisms of sonocrystallisation in solution
coredata.dc:creator: Nalesso, Silvia
Bussemaker, Madeleine J.
Sear, Richard P.
Hodnett, Mark
Lee, Judy
coredata.dc:description: Sonocrystallisation is the application of ultrasound to the crystallisation process. The benefits obtained by sonication have been widely studied since the beginning of the 20th century and so far it is clear that ultrasound can be a very useful tool for enhancing crystallisation and controlling the properties of the final product. Crystal size, polymorphs, purity, process repeatability and lower induction time are only some of the advantages of sonocrystallisation. Even though the effects of sonication on crystallisation are quite clear, the physical explanation of the phenomena involved is still lacking. Is the presence of cavitation necessary for the process? Or is only the bubbles surface responsible for enhancing crystallisation? Are the strong local increases in pressure and temperature induced by cavitation the main cause of all the observed effects? Or is it the strong turbulence induced in the system instead? Many questions still remain and can only be appreciated with an understanding of the complexity behind the individual processes of crystallisation and acoustic cavitation. Therefore, this review will first summarise the theories behind crystallisation and acoustic cavitation, followed by a description of all the current proposed sonocrystallisation mechanisms, and conclude with an overview on future prospects of sonocrystallisation applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sonocrystallization mechanisms
Acoustic cavitation
Crystal nucleation
Sono-deagglomeration
Sono-agglomeration
Sono-fragmentation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Supersaturation ratio under extreme bubble pressure and temperature
Crystal clusters formation under acoustic pressure gradients (segregation theory)
Critical free energy barrier and mass diffusion in ultrasound

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical diagram of solubility expressed in concentration as function of temperature and the different regions of saturation. The process lines from A to B and A to B* indicate the moving into the supersaturated regime at constant concentration and temperature, respectively. Points B and B* indicate the intersection of the process with the solubility line. The metastable region is defined as the conditions where the solution is supersaturated but where nucleation is found to either very slow or not to occur at all. Reprinted from [44] with permission of the American Chemical Society, Copyright 2001.

Diagram of overall free energy for a crystallisation process, showing the point of maximum energy that corresponds to the minimum radius at which nuclei start growing. Reprinted from [50] with permission of the American Chemical Society, Copyright 2009.
Summary of cavitation activity steps along the time: active bubble formation, growth and implosion.

Sodium chloride crystals made in anti-solvent crystallisation, comparing effect of mixing speed (top) and sonication under different frequencies (bottom). The white bar in each image indicates 20 μm. Adapted from Ref. [14].

Liquid pressure (top) and temperature (bottom) around the bubble immediately after the collapse, when 29 kHz is applied to the system. R0 is the initial bubble radius before the collapse. Reprinted from [72] with permission of the American Chemical Society, Copyright 2016.

Diagram of Segregation Theory. In the starting solution, molecules of solute are present in the system. When sonication is applied, cavitating bubbles are formed in the system. Since the molecules of solute are small, i.e. low-density particles, they are not segregated by the cavitating bubble and they stay close to the bubble wall. The molecules start forming clusters that are over-concentrated at the bubble wall, increasing the probability of meeting each other and other solute molecules. When they reach their final crystal size, i.e. they become high-density particles, they are expelled from the cavitating bubble zone into the bulk.
Schematic representation of secondary nucleation in presence of a bubble collapsing. Starting from the initial crystal, clusters and nuclei are formed.
Diagram representing the different ways in which sonication can affect the crystal growth: a) sonofragmentation, b) sono-deagglomeration and c) crystal sono-agglomeration.

Images showing sono-agglomeration of BaTiO3 mesocrystals from Dang et al., in particular a) SEM image of crystals [143] (Copyright 2009 The Japan Society of Applied Physics), and b) TEM image of a crystal [145] (Copyright 2010 Ultrasonics Sonochemistry), both obtained after 20 min of sonication.

Images of transient cavitation causing sonocrystallisation, in particular crystals forming from non-symmetrical transient cavitation of Lee et al. Reprinted from [155] with permission of the American Chemical Society, Copyright 2018.
Summary of the mechanism proposed in the present review.

row.entry:
Critical Free Energy
Δ G
crit
Erosion, shattering and disruption of nuclei

head.ce:title: A review on possible mechanisms of sonocrystallisation in solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Process Engineering, University of Surrey, Guildford, Surrey GU2 7XH, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Process Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Surrey, Guildford, Surrey GU2 7XH, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Physical Laboratory, Teddington, Middlesex TW11 0LW, United Kingdom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sonocrystallisation is the application of ultrasound to the crystallisation process. The benefits obtained by sonication have been widely studied since the beginning of the 20th century and so far it is clear that ultrasound can be a very useful tool for enhancing crystallisation and controlling the properties of the final product. Crystal size, polymorphs, purity, process repeatability and lower induction time are only some of the advantages of sonocrystallisation. Even though the effects of sonication on crystallisation are quite clear, the physical explanation of the phenomena involved is still lacking. Is the presence of cavitation necessary for the process? Or is only the bubbles surface responsible for enhancing crystallisation? Are the strong local increases in pressure and temperature induced by cavitation the main cause of all the observed effects? Or is it the strong turbulence induced in the system instead? Many questions still remain and can only be appreciated with an understanding of the complexity behind the individual processes of crystallisation and acoustic cavitation. Therefore, this review will first summarise the theories behind crystallisation and acoustic cavitation, followed by a description of all the current proposed sonocrystallisation mechanisms, and conclude with an overview on future prospects of sonocrystallisation applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Crystallisation is an important unit operation used in many different industrial processes. Pharmaceutical, food and chemicals are some examples where the control of crystal size and shape, as well as polymorphs, is important due to its impact on product stability, solubility, flowability and bioavailability [1–4]. The application of ultrasound (US) during a crystallisation process is called sonocrystallisation. The first attempt of sonocrystallisation was in 1927 by Richard and Loomis [5], but the results obtained were limited by the technology available at the time. As new and more stable piezo devices and better analytical tools are discovered, research into potential applications for the ultrasound technology multiplied. It was in the 1950s and 1970s in the former Soviet Union that the possibility of using US to obtain smaller and more uniform size crystals was demonstrated [6–8]. During the following years, many studies were carried out to further show the benefits of sonocrystallisation: higher crystal purity, improved product homogeneity, faster crystallisation time, preferential polymorph formation and better process repeatability [1–3,9–25]

A range of successful applications of US on an industrial scale have been reported or patented [26]: US coupled with droplet atomization for the crystallisation of paracetamol to improve the control of size, sphericity and surface topography [27]; US coupled with a vortex mixer for obtaining crystals smaller than 5 µm [28]; production of drug powder for inhalation in a continuous process [29]; crystallisation of triglyceride oil using US to prevent flavour deterioration [30]; enhancing crystallisation of impurities for easy removal during alumina production from bauxite (Bayer process) [31]. US has also been successfully trialled in a number of continuous systems such as an oscillatory baffled crystallizer [32], slug-flow cooling crystallizer [33], millifluidic channel crystallizer [34,35] and droplet-based capillary sonocrystallizer [36].

Although the effects of US on crystallisation have been widely studied, there is still no consensus on the fundamental mechanism of US action on the crystallisation process. This is largely due to the short time scales (down to picoseconds [37,38]) and lengths (down to nm) in which crystallisation and cavitation occur. Therefore, the majority of studies attempt to probe the mechanism by measuring bulk properties or analysing the phenomena at macroscale. This often leads to ambiguous results because the instruments used are not designed for monitoring extreme rapid phenomena [24,39]. In addition, US is very sensitive to the process conditions that can interfere and alter the pressure wave field, i.e. reactor geometry, material of the reactor wall, height of the liquid, dissolved gas concentration, temperature and presence of surface active agents or solids in the system [40–43]. Crystallisation itself is also very sensitive to process conditions. Due to the dependence on so many process parameters such as temperature, solute concentration, mixing and impurities, it is often very difficult to totally replicate a sonocrystallisation system in different laboratories. This is further complicated by the variation in sonication parameters being studied and reported: frequency, power, type of transducer (horn and plate) and sonication time, making it difficult to compare between different studies

This review will provide the theoretical background behind the individual processes, namely crystallisation and acoustic cavitation, followed by an overview of the explanations proposed in the literature for describing sonocrystallisation mechanism.

Crystallisation is a separation process in which a solute precipitates from the solvent, forming a two phase solid-liquid system. For crystallisation to occur, supersaturation needs to be established first, followed by crystal nucleation and growth [44]. This process is governed mainly by the solute’s solubility and a typical solubility diagram with the regions of saturation and supersaturation is shown in Fig. 1 . The solubility line describes the thermodynamic limit for the solute to remain in solution, while the nucleation line represents the points in which crystal nuclei are formed spontaneously. Supersaturation is usually described as a concentration ratio or relative supersaturation with respect to the equilibrium saturation concentration indicated by the solubility line. The zone between the nucleation and the solubility curves is called the metastable zone (MSZ).

In order to crystallise the solute at point A shown in Fig. 1, it is necessary to change the system condition to destabilise the equilibrium. This can be achieved by decreasing the temperature of the system along the horizontal line from point A, until the line intersects the solubility line at point B. However, no crystals will appear until the temperature reaches point C on the nucleation line, where spontaneous nucleation occurs. Another method of crystallising solute from point A is by changing the concentration along the vertical line from A to B*. This can be achieved by evaporation or by adding a second solvent where the solute has a lower solubility. The latter process is generally known as anti-solvent crystallisation, but considering the conditions in which the anti-solvent is added, it is possible to distinguish between drowning-out and salting-out precipitation. Drowning-out is when the solution is added to the second solvent, while salting-out is when the second solvent is added to the solution [45].

There are two types of nucleation, primary and secondary. Primary nucleation occurs in the absence of other crystalline structures in the system, whereas secondary nucleation occurs when crystals of the solute, either from seeds or primary nucleation, are present in the system [44]. In a crystallisation from a homogeneous solution, secondary nucleation follows after primary nucleation, and it needs a lower level of supersaturation with respect to primary nucleation. Seeding crystallisation is based on secondary nucleation processes, providing particles with controlled size and shape inside the system at the beginning of the process in order to crystallise readily [44,46]. A diagram of the nucleation classifications is reported in Fig. 2 .

If the solution has no impurities and is not in contact with a surface (e.g. of a container) that promotes nucleation, primary nucleation will be homogenous, but in the presence of foreign particles, heterogeneous nucleation occurs [48,49]. Impurities can accelerate or inhibit the crystallisation, depending on the interfacial tension between the crystalline solid and the other particles [44]. Homogenous nucleation is rare because processes are seldom contaminant and surface free [45]. The type of nucleation also depends on the supersaturation degree: for a low degree of supersaturation heterogeneous nucleation is usually observed, while for a high degree of supersaturation homogenous nucleation can be dominant [46]. The most common theoretical model to describe primary nucleation is the classical nucleation theory (CNT).

Under the CNT, in the case of homogenous nucleation the crystal nuclei are formed by density fluctuations inside the solution, formation of crystal clusters from collisions and addition of molecules. The excess free energy of cluster formation is denoted ΔG, which is the sum of the surface excess free energy, ΔGS, necessary for creating the surface, and the volume excess free energy, ΔGV, which is related to the formation of nuclei volume [44]:
(2.1.1)
Δ G = Δ
G S
+ Δ
G V
= 4 π
r
2
γ +
4 3
π
r
3
Δ
G v
where r is the radius of the nuclei, γ is the interfacial tension between the particles of solute and in the supersaturated solution, ΔG v is the free energy change per unit of volume. The two terms in Eq. (2.1.1) depend on r in different ways, as it is also shown in Fig. 3 .

The overall ΔG has a maximum value at ΔGcrit when the size of nuclei reaches the critical radius (rcrit), and within this theory, it is this maximum energy barrier that determines the nucleation rate. Crystals smaller than this rcrit will dissolve away, whereas crystals larger than rcrit will grow in size.

According to the CNT, the rate of nucleation in a homogeneous nucleation, J, is defined by [44]:
(2.1.2)
J = A ∙ e x p
-
16 π
γ
3
v
2
3
k
3
T
3
(
ln S )
2
where A is a pre-exponential factor, ν is the molecular volume, k is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature and S is the degree of supersaturation. Theoretically, increasing the supersaturation S and decreasing the temperature T would cause the rate of nucleation J to increase rapidly, but experimental results show that there is a maximum supersaturation ratio and a minimum temperature that can improve nucleation, while beyond these values the process is inhibited [44].

Experimentally, the nucleation rate can be determined by measuring the induction time, which is the time required between the onset of supersaturation and the first appearance of detectable crystals [44]. Following the assumptions that the transient period during the nucleation stage is negligible and that the nucleation time is much higher than the growth time, the induction time (tind) in a homogenous nucleation can be calculated as the inverse of the nucleation rate [51]:
(2.1.3)
t
ind
=
J
s
- 1

The expression in Eq. (2.1.2), after being rearranged as per Eq. (2.1.3), relates the induction time with the supersaturation ratio [51]:
(2.1.4)
t ind
∝ exp
B
(
ln S )
2
γ
3
16 π
γ
3
v
2
3
k
3
T
3
(
ln S )
2
where B is a constant. Therefore, the induction time is predicted to be very sensitive to the surface free energy of the crystal in solution, γ , and the supersaturation, S.

Although the CNT is still considered as the basic theory for crystallisation, it is not applicable to real systems, since the conditions of a homogeneous nucleation are difficult to obtain. Therefore, the following semi-empirical equation is generally accepted for describing a crystallisation process [44]:
(2.1.5)
J =
k n
Δ
c
max
n
where kn is the nucleation rate constant, Δcmax is the metastable zone width and n is the apparent order of nucleation. Hence, crystallisation can simply be considered as a reaction and the order of nucleation has the same role as the order of reaction, usually inferred empirically [44]. Considering the very general form of this equation, it can be applied to all types of crystallisation. The process is described macroscopically, ignoring all the microscopic phenomena such as the interfacial tension or the change in the free energy, all included inside the nucleation rate constant and the order of nucleation.

It is important to highlight that nucleation almost always occurs at a surface, such as the surface of the vessel used for the crystallisation or of an impurity, as well as the interface between different phases [49,52]. Therefore, heterogeneous nucleation is the one commonly observed, while there are only a few examples of homogeneous nucleation proved by experimental data in the literature [53–56]. In fact, all real systems are confined, i.e. they have surfaces in which nucleation can occur, and the elimination of all the impurities is almost impossible. However, homogeneous nucleation is used to elucidate the fundamental processes, therefore it is usually presented when crystallisation is explained theoretically.

The fact that the CNT cannot properly describe crystallisation is widely known among the experts in the field [50,52,57,58]. The CNT is essentially an extension of the theory introduced by Gibbs at the end of the 19th century for explaining the formation of another fluid in a fluid phase. This theory mainly focused on liquid-vapour systems, even if the fundamentals could have been applied to all types of biphasic systems. When the necessity of describing crystallisation using equations arose, scientists found easier to start from the Gibb’s theory and its assumptions. However, many years of experiments rarely confirmed the CNT because the majority of real systems cannot respect the postulates required. One example is the assumption of a one-step process. In many cases the formation of a metastable phase followed by the crystallisation of the final product has been observed, as reviewed by Sear [52]. This metastable phase can be another polymorph or an intermediate phase of the liquid. Different studies showed that nucleation occurs in more than one step, one subsequent the other, such as for lysozyme [59], polymers [60], urea and glycine [61], and organic materials [62]. New non-classical theories have been proposed [57], but more work is still needed for describing the complexity of the nucleation step.

It can be useful instead to point out some important features about nucleation, observed in both experimental and modelling works. First of all, as claimed in the previous section, nucleation mainly occurs at surfaces and interfaces, often those of impurities. CNT predicts that this is because the free energy barrier to nucleation is lower at surfaces [48]. But it is still difficult to predict how these surfaces affect nuclei, because it is almost impossible to know precisely which sort of impurities are inside the system, or the exact characteristics of the container surface, or the interfaces formed during the process and how they transform [52].

The nucleation rate is also sensitive to many other different factors, like presence of dirt, thermal history of the solution and the nucleation surface structure [48,52]. Different impurities can have different affinity with the nuclei of the crystal, therefore they can either enhance or hinder nucleation. In addition, especially when the impurities have a different size from the solute molecules, strains in the lattice can be formed, sometimes decreasing or even stopping the growth [52]. On the other hand, the thermal history of a solution can actually change its composition, removing or transforming impurities, eliminating bubbles that can act as nucleating surface, and so on [48,63]. Furthermore, presence of wedges, curved surfaces, pores and pits can enhance or inhibit nucleation basing on the match between crystal and surface structures [52].

Finally, the nucleation rate is very sensitive to supersaturation, i.e. a small change in supersaturation can have a significant effect on the nucleation rate, predicted by the CNT and proved experimentally [48,64]. This behaviour is one of the reasons behind the inherent randomness of nucleation, which causes two identical samples to crystallise at different times because the supersaturation fluctuations do not happen at the same time, and a very small change in the solute concentration has a substantial effect on nucleation [48].

Therefore, it is clear that even if crystallisation is a very common unit operation, we are still far from its complete knowledge and understanding. The factors affecting the formation of nuclei have been detected through both experiments and simulations, but the nucleation rates still remain unpredictable. The models used up to now are too simple for including all the possible parameters that can influence nucleation [48].

Secondary nucleation is the formation of new nuclei or new clusters through different mechanisms such as contact nucleation, fluid shear, displacement of the solute layer on the crystal surface and presence of fine particles/fragments [44,45,47]. Contact nucleation is when nucleation starts after collision of seed crystals against the vessel, impeller or other crystals, and is considered as the main secondary nucleation mechanism [65]. Furthermore, fluid shear can exert mechanical shear forces onto the crystal surface, forming new nuclei [47]. Another mechanism is the displacement of the adsorbed solute layer near the crystal surface, causing coagulation of the solute clusters, i.e. producing the new nuclei [47]. Finally, new sources of nuclei can be fine particles on the surface of the seeds, presence of needle or dendrite growths that can be detached from the main crystal, or the formation of fragments from the parent crystals due to collisions phenomena [47]. However, it is important to underline that the exact mechanisms behind secondary nucleation are still not totally clear [47,66,67]. This represents a big limitation because the dominating nucleation process in industry is essentially secondary nucleation. Seed crystallisation is very often used in practice, because it is an easy and cheap technique for controlling crystal size and polymorphs.

The majority of substances that crystallise exhibit polymorphism, which is the formation of crystals with the same chemical composition, but different lattices. Therefore, structure of the crystals differ and this can also affect their physical properties, such as density, hardness, melting point, solubility, reactivity and bioavailability etc. [44]. Polymorphs should not be confused with solvates or hydrates (which are chemically different) or with enantiomorphs (which have same solubility and melting points) [44]. The thermodynamic stability of the different polymorphs can vary depending on the temperature, pressure and solvent used, this can cause polymorphs to transform from one to another during crystallisation [44]. As a consequence, a specific polymorph can be formed at the beginning of the crystallisation, but if it is metastable, it will rapidly transform to a more stable form. Predicting which polymorphs could be formed during a crystallisation and which one is the most stable in the conditions studied is challenging, and is mainly conducted experimentally [68]. However, some modelling attempts for polymorphism were successful, even if these are still used to support experimental results [69].

Ultrasound is usually generated by piezoelectric ceramic transducers with a certain operating frequency. For horn type transducers (usually 20–100 kHz), it concentrates the sound energy at the tip of the horn, causing localised cavitation and high fluid movement to develop close to the tip [70]. For plate type transducers (20 kHz–2 MHz), the surface area for the emission of the sound wave is larger, diameter around 5.0 cm, allowing a further wave propagation through the entire bulk of the liquid. For these reasons, the horn is usually used when high concentration of energy in a small area is required and for small liquid volumes, while the plate transducer is useful for larger volumes and for having a more homogenous wave field compared to the horn.

When US waves propagate in a liquid, the variation in the pressures create the cavitation phenomena, which is the formation, growth and collapse of bubbles (Fig. 4 ) [70]. It is well accepted that cavitation bubbles originate from pre-existing gas nuclei in the liquid, as experiments have shown that if the liquid is degassed or filtered, the cavitation activity decreases and cavitation threshold is increased [71]. These gas nuclei then oscillate and grow in solution by the action of the oscillating pressure sound waves until it reaches the linear resonance size (active size), when it undergoes inertial collapse [37]. The collapse of the bubble causes rapid adiabatic compression of gases and vapours [70] inside the bubble, and generates very high pressure (up to tens of GPa) and temperature (up to tens of thousands K) [72], as well as shockwaves, microjets and micro-turbulences [73]. Cavitation bubbles that undergoes repetitive inertial collapse are classified as stable cavitation bubbles, whereas bubbles that are short lived or collapses in a few acoustic cycles are termed transient cavitation bubbles [35,74–77].

The collapse intensity, active bubble size and population of bubbles at the active size are dependent on the sonication frequency, power and solution conditions. Cavitation bubbles generated at higher frequencies are usually stable cavitation bubbles, whereas at lower frequencies the cavitation are usually transient [78–80]. The general understanding is that the size of the active bubble decreases with increasing frequency [81], therefore the collapse intensity would be much lower at higher frequencies compared to the lower frequencies. In addition, the number of active bubbles would increase as a function of frequency due to the increased number of antinodes being established in the solution [82]. The balance between the change in the size and population of active bubbles as a function of frequency is used to explain the optimum frequency observed for a given application. However, the bubble collapse intensity, size and population are also affected by the liquid height, reactor vessel geometry and nature of the solution because these can further affect the sound field established in the liquid [71]. This makes it difficult to draw comparative sonication studies from different systems as well as by different research groups.

Due to the speed and short time scales which both crystallisation and cavitation occurs, measurable variables such as induction time and crystal size distribution are commonly used to examine the effect of sonocrystallisation. Typically observed effects are: a decrease in the metastable zone-width and induction time, reduced crystal size and narrower size distribution, better control of nucleation rate and reproducibility, and polymorph selectivity. However, some studies [83,84] have shown exceptions with opposite results.

It is possible that the intense mixing induced by US increases the opportunity of collisions of solute molecules, and allows them to penetrate the stagnant film adjacent to the crystal and “jump” into the crystal lattice more easily. However, the effects of mixing are different from the effects induced by US, with the crystals obtained under sonicated conditions being superior in terms of uniformity with respect to size and shape [14,73,85], as it is shown in Fig. 5 . Crystals obtained with sonication are more regular, and no agglomeration has been detected.

The mechanisms behind sonocrystallisation are still debated and finding a univocal conclusion appears to be difficult. Nevertheless, various fundamental mechanisms behind this phenomenon have been suggested and are summarised in the following sections. The theories have been grouped based on which crystallisation step is affected, which are primary and secondary nucleation, and growth and fragmentation. For primary nucleation, homogeneous and heterogeneous nucleation have been split, listing the effects on the supersaturation ratio, on clusters formation, on the critical free energy and on the Mass Diffusion, as part of the Homogenous Nucleation. The review then moves to the Secondary Nucleation and Growth. For this last section, Sonofragmentation, Deagglomeration and Crystal agglomeration phenomena are described.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Supersaturation ratio under extreme bubble pressure and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Solubility usually increases with temperature, therefore the influence of US on crystallisation is counter-intuitive as the high temperatures generated inside the bubble would lead to a decrease in the supersaturation. The increase in solubility of solutes by US have been reported for sodium sulphide and calcium citrate [86], where sonication under isothermal conditions resulted in a 1.4 fold increase in the solubility concentration compared to the equilibrium saturation values. On the other hand, it is also true that the high pressure generated by cavitation bubbles can enhance the solidification of solutes and improve crystallisation processes. However, it is unclear how US enhanced the observed crystallisation of K2SO4 [87] where the solute’s solubility increases with pressure, as well for ZnSO4·7H2O [88] where the solubility is independent of pressure. It is important to underline that bulk temperature and pressure of a system do not change dramatically during a sonocrystallisation, and that the localised extreme conditions occur in a time scale of picoseconds during the bubble collapse phase, near the minimum bubble size [89].

Although extreme temperatures can be reached inside a collapsing bubble, very rapid undercooling of the adjacent liquid during bubble collapse can also occur, and this can enhance nucleation. The undercooling is attributed to the evaporation of solvent from bubble surface towards the core as the bubble expands, thereby filling the cavity with vapour and cooling the liquid on the surface at the same time [90]. Although cooling rates of the order 107-1010 K/s were reported [31], studies effectively shown that the solvent evaporation does not change the temperature enough for inducing nucleation [91], nor does it alter the supersaturation degree at the interfacial area [92]. However, the hypothesis of a metastable state of subcooling in the liquid near the bubble wall during the final stages of bubble collapse was considered by Hickling and co-workers [93,94]. During this metastable state a transient, high-pressure solidification can occur, initiating the crystallisation.

Some authors have found that nucleation occurs during the bubble collapse, and not during the growth of a cavity. Hunt and Jackson [91] attributed this to the effect of pressure on the solute’s melting temperature. As the pressure alternates locally between large positive and negative change in pressure, the melting temperature can also change and induce crystal nucleation once the system temperature coincides with it [93,95]. However, this theory does not consider the possible dissolution of the solid once it is formed. Furthermore, numerical simulations of cavitation did not show any negative change in pressure being induced in the liquid immediately outside the bubbles after the collapse [72,96], nor further away from the bubbles [95].

It is important to underline that the major problem is the impossibility to measure directly the rapid and localised increase in temperature and pressure during the bubble collapse. This makes the understanding of the effects even more cryptic. Attempt to indirectly measure experimentally temperature and pressure of the gas inside a cavitating bubble has been done by Suslick et al. through kinetic measurements and sonoluminescence spectra [97–100]. For a multi bubble system a temperature around 5000 K and a minimum pressure of 30 MPa have been detected in a solution of Cr(CO)6 in silicone oil saturated with argon [98,100]. However, the temperature and pressure in the liquid side immediately adjacent to the bubble still remains unknown. Through simulation, Cogné et al. [72] calculated the local and surrounding temperature and pressure of liquid water with air bubbles, before and after the bubble collapse at 29 kHz. The results showed that, immediately after the collapse, the liquid around the collapsed bubble can reach up to 650 K and a pressure of more than 10 GPa (Fig. 6 ). Since it was observed that temperature is quick to return to ambient conditions than pressure after the collapse, it means that there is a region around the bubble that is at room temperature but high pressure. Therefore, in this region the conditions can be suitable for starting the crystallisation.

The first attempt made to numerically correlate the collapse pressure of cavitation bubbles with the crystal nucleation rate has been done in 2006 by Virone et al. [39]. The model can calculate the pressure peak upon bubble collapse and it correlates with the nucleation rate. From the nucleation rate it is possible to calculate the induction time, which is usually measured in crystallisation experiments. However, although a reduction in the induction time with sonication was calculated by the model, when comparing to that measured from the experiments, there is a difference of 5 orders of magnitude. Nevertheless, the developed model would be helpful to understand whether the application of US would be useful for a specific system, since a clear reduction on the induction time with sonication can be calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal clusters formation under acoustic pressure gradients (segregation theory)
ce:section.ce:para: A group of researchers [88,101–103] developed a segregation theory based on the “pressure diffusion” phenomenon. It is a mechanical effect, usually caused by high pressure-gradients and it is the basis of ultracentrifugation, used for separating particles from a fluid. It can be explained by considering a fluid containing particles of different sizes and densities. If a difference in pressure is induced, the fluid will flow. This fluid motion will then cause the particles to segregate: larger and heavier particles will move with the fluid due to their inertia and drift velocity, while smaller and lighter particles will have a near-zero movement because their buoyancy force is higher than their inertia, so an opposite effect is obtained. In the case of acoustic cavitation, high pressure differences are induced, therefore it is possible that a segregation phenomenon takes place among the solute molecules present in the solution [102]. It was shown theoretically [103] that pressure gradients generated by an oscillating bubble could allow small solute molecules to build up adjacent to an oscillating bubble wall. These solutes can then cluster and reach the critical cluster size necessary for spontaneous crystal nucleation, resulting in the observed sonocrystallisation. When the solute clusters reach a large enough size, they are then pushed away from the oscillating bubble and into the bulk phase (Fig. 7 ).

Therefore, US seems capable of segregating particles of different sizes, resulting in a balance between agglomeration of the clusters and removal of the crystals formed with their final size from the bubble. As a consequence, crystallisation is enhanced and more controlled due to the action of the pressure induced by cavitation bubbles. The segregation theory was introduced to better explain the complex mechanisms behind the application of US in crystallisation. Modelling experimental data showed the over-concentration of clusters around the critical nucleus of collapsing bubbles, but the theory still remains semi-quantitative [88].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Critical free energy barrier and mass diffusion in ultrasound
ce:section.ce:para: Considering Eq. (2.1.2), there are two main terms on which US could act: the overall critical free energy and the pre-exponential factor. Some studies showed that sonication can provide the energy (ΔGcrit) needed to overcome the critical energetic barrier required for spontaneous nucleation to occur [10,84,104,105]. Indeed, the critical free energy required for starting nucleation is in the order of 10−20 J [49] and the energy produced by a collapsing bubble has been calculated to be in the order of 10−3 J [106]. This means that US could potentially give more than enough energy to the system for inducing crystallisation.

A 2006 study looked into the existence of a relationship between the minimum amount of US energy supplied to the system and the supersaturation ratio for nucleation [10,104]. It was observed that for the cooling crystallisation of acetylsalicylic acid at low US power, an increase in particle size was detected as a function of irradiation time. With increasing power, the average particle size decreased with irradiation time, as commonly observed in many other systems [10]. When the supersaturation ratio was decreased, a higher power was required to cause the same decrease in particle size. It was concluded that there is a relationship between the energy required to form a stable nuclei (which depends on the supersaturation ratio), and the energy required to produce a higher number of crystals than in silent conditions [10,104]. As a consequence of this, there are saturation regions in which US can activate the primary nucleation and others in which it inhibits the process [104]. A physical mechanism that can explain the inhibition has not been presented. One possible theory is related to the US breakage of the primary nuclei formed in the system. Sonication is known to cause fragmentation and erosion (discussed in Sec. 4.3.1). Therefore, US could break or erode the nuclei that have reached the critical size and with the nuclei now smaller than the critical size, it will tend to dissolve (Fig. 2) leading to the observed inhibition of nulceation by US.

In another study on ice nucleation in a single bubble system, it was concluded that a minimum acoustic pressure (or energy) of about 0.6 bar was required to initiate crystallisation [107]. In addition, images captured showed that ice nucleation does not always occur after just one acoustic cycle. Hence it is possible that a continuously oscillating bubble is required for crystallisation, another demonstration that a minimum energy level given by the multiple oscillations should be reached for initiating crystallisation [108].

Ultrasound also influences polymorphism, which can be related to the amount of energy supplied to the system. The formation of polymorphs is mainly regulated by the thermodynamics, and for each system studied the more stable polymorph is the one with the lower free energy at a given temperature [44]. Even if one polymorph is kinetically promoted, but not thermodynamically stable, it will most probably transform into another polymorph with a lower free energy. Therefore, US can effectively promote polymorphs that are not usually stable at the specific temperature of crystallisation. For example, the β-form of p-Aminobenzoic Acid was produced with a 20 kHz horn at a temperature in which this polymorph is metastable [109]. Similarly the α-form of glycine, increased in purity when sonicated with an US horn [110]. In addition, different combinations of US intensities, sonication times and temperature produced different proportions of the three main polymorphs of calcium carbonate: vaterite, calcite and aragonite [111]. Finally, a systematic study on the anti-solvent crystallisation of paracetamol showed that with a plate transducer whether an increase on frequency up to 98 kHz or an increment of the acoustic power can enhance the production of form II [3]. Although the exact explanation behind these effects is still not available, it is certain that US can effectively alter the energy of the system, promoting polymorphs that are usually energetically unstable or metastable in silent conditions.

Alternatively, sonication can affect the pre-exponential factor A in Eq. (2.1.2) through the enhancement of the mass diffusion in the system. In a study on the anti-solvent crystallisation of roxithromycin [2], Guo et al. measured the variation of the induction time in function of the supersaturation ratio. Fitting the experimental data and assuming that the surface tension does not change using US, a 3.82-fold increase in the diffusion coefficient was calculated when the system was sonicated, demonstrating that the mass transfer was effectively improved. Interestingly, this study showed that homogenous nucleation was detected with and without US, therefore excluding the possibility of sonication changing the nucleation mechanism from heterogeneous to homogeneous.

However, the homogenous nucleation theory is controversial as in practice it is very difficult to have homogenous nucleation since almost all systems are contaminated by impurities [44,85]. In 1963 an attempt of explaining how US can induce homogenous nucleation introduced the concept of “micro-inhomogeneity” [112]. It was hypothesised that a system could be considered homogenous from the macroscopic point of view, but could have micro-inhomogeneity responsible for inducing nucleation [112]. Sonication could then redistribute the micronuclei and change the probability of spontaneous nucleation [112]. The introduction of the concept of micro-inhomogeneities in the system clearly shows the fragility of the homogeneous theory for US-induced nucleation.

Another theory is based on the idea that US can change the nucleation path from homogenous to heterogeneous. This mechanism is considered by many authors because heterogeneous nucleation occurs in the presence of phase boundaries. Reactor surfaces and impurities can decrease the effective surface energy for crystal nucleation [85] and lower the activated energy barrier for nucleation compared to a homogeneous nucleation. Therefore, the dramatic decrease in the observed induction time when the system is sonicated could be attributed to the cavitation bubble surfaces acting as nucleation sites by decreasing the effective surface energy. This lowering of the interfacial tension term thus causies the nucleation rate to increase.

One of the first studies made in this context was by Hem [19], who showed that the crystallisation temperature in a cooling crystallisation decreases as the number of foreign particles in the solution is reduced, both in silent and sonicated conditions (20 kHz). Similarly, an increase of US power decreased the apparent order of nucleation by lowering the activation energy, thus allowing crystallisation to occur at a higher temperature [87]. Modelling of US-induced cooling crystallisation has confirmed this by comparing simulation results to experimental data [113].

The heterogeneous nucleation mechanism suggests that active cavitation bubbles are not necessary for sonocrystallisation, since the presence of non cavitating bubbles in the system can enhance nucleation [114,115]. For example, in a cooling crystallisation of supercooled water, the degree of supercooling was higher when the conditions allowed to have a higher content of bubble nuclei dissolved in the water [116]. Another work on the cooling crystallisation of dodecandioc acid (DDDA) by Wohlgemuth [92] has shown that gassing the system with air bubbles can reduce the MSZW and increasing the amount of smaller crystals, with bimodal PSD (Particle Size Distribution). For the anti-solvent crystallisation of NaCl in EtOH, the average crystal size was 7 µm after 1 min of N2 gassing, compared to 13 µm obtained without the addition of N2 minute bubbles for the same crystallisation time [117]. Finally, for the anti-solvent sonocrystallisation of KCl (20 kHz, 500 W), argon was injected simultaneously with sonication [73]. The results showed that the addition of argon increased the number of nuclei respect to the sole sonication since both cavitation activity and convection were enhanced, but the growth rate was not affected, resulting in smaller and more homogenous crystals. A control study on the system has also been done with mechanical stirring and in this case the PSD obtained is very similar to the one obtained with sonication only. In addition, both nucleation and growth rate calculated with mixing are of the same order of magnitude of the ones estimated in case of US. Injection of gas and even sonication can cause additional fluid mixing that can lead to a reduction in the PSD (Fig. 5) [14] and therefore, influence of mechanical mixing for a given system and careful interpretation of the results are needed.

The US-induced secondary nucleation is based on the idea that sonication causes erosion or abrasion of existing crystals [118]. These phenomena can be responsible for the formation of clusters and new nuclei, as it is shown in Fig. 8 . Hem [19] in 1967 reported that for the cooling crystallisation of hydrocortisone, sonication at 20 kHz did not initiate crystallisation above the temperature required under silent conditions, concluding that sonication does not trigger nucleation. The decrease in the final crystal size and the increase on crystal number in sonicated conditions was therefore attributed to erosion, shattering and disruption of crystal nuclei, which normally appear in a solution [19]. Similarly, Gielen et al. [16] found a reduction in the crystal size was only obtained if US was applied after induction time and not before, therefore suggesting that US enhanced secondary nucleation by disaggregation or sonofragmentation of existing crystals. In addition, for calcium carbonate precipitation, the main influence of US on the PSD was during the early growth step, immediately after the nucleation, suggesting that the formation of smaller crystals is related to deagglomeration phenomena on the primary clusters [119]. Finally, deagglomeration of clusters was captured via a high-speed camera where images clearly showed the breakage of clusters from bubble oscillations [120,121].

After crystal nucleation has occurred, the nuclei can grow or agglomerate, causing the crystal size to increase. On the other hand, the crystals can reduce in size via fragmentation or deagglomeration of aggregates. The effect of ultrasound on these effects are denote here as sono-fragmentation, sono-deagglomeration and sonoagglomeration (Fig. 9 ).

The physical effects created by the inertial bubble collapse can cause fragmentation or erosion of the already formed crystals, known as sonofragmentation [122–135]. This would reduce the crystal size produced from a top down approach, therefore influencing the final size of the crystals through breakage and erosion phenomena, as it is shown in Fig. 9a.

In several reported studies, US seems to enhance the inter-particle collisions, considered as the origin of the observed metallic particle fragmentation [122,128–130,132] and attributed to the turbulent flow and shock waves generated by the US field. However, this inter-particle collision does not explain the observed fragmentation rate of molecular crystals, which have different physical properties compared to metallic crystals (e.g. density, tensile strength, friability) [134]. Zeiger et al. [134] isolated the different types of collisions (inter-particles, particle-transducer, particle-wall and particle-shockwave collisions) using a molecular crystal, acetylsalicylic acid, concluding that the primary mechanism for fragmentation under high intensity sonication was particle-shockwave interactions [134].

Sonofragmentation was analysed in detail by Jordens et al. [125], where the final crystal size of paracetamol crystals were analysed after 3 h sonication at different frequencies (30–1140 kHz) and powers (10–30 W). It was observed that the decrease in the particle size essentially depends on the type of bubbles that are generated by US in the system. The type of cavitation has been determined through sonoluminescence quenching experiments, a technique previously reported [80,136]. Transient cavitation was detected at 30 kHz, while between 41 kHz and 1.14 MHz the cavitation was stable. The results showed that for frequencies with predominant stable cavitation the particle breakage reaches a minimum size threshold with increasing power, i.e. further increase in power had very little effect on decreasing the particle size. This was attributed to the stable bubbles become larger and long-lived at high power levels, therefore they can accumulate a larger amount of gas inside, causing cushioning effects during the bubble collapse. Since the collapse strength is limited, the crystal breakage is also limited, causing a minimum particle size threshold to be reached that is independent of the initial crystal size, frequency and power. On the other hand, if transient cavitation was predominant, no minimum size threshold was detected and the median particle size decreased sharply with increasing power. This was attributed to the lower amount of gas being accumulated in the bubbles compared to the case of stable cavitation bubbles. In addition, for a fixed calorimetric power, increasing frequency decreased the particle size until 166 kHz, and a further increase in frequency resulted in no further particle breakage. This trend was explained by variations in bubble size: low frequencies create larger bubbles that collapse violently, generating strong shockwaves, while high frequencies generate smaller bubbles that implode less violently. As a consequence, at low frequencies the fragmentation is stronger than at higher frequencies.

The physical effects of US can also deagglomerate crystals (Fig. 9b) [121,137–141] that would normally agglomerate under silent conditions from the formation of strong bonds among crystals through liquid bridges. The application of US has shown to reduce the agglomeration of crystals [137]. Agglomerates are very difficult to break, unlike aggregates that are kept together by weak forces between the single crystals [137]. Agglomeration is often a disadvantage due to impurities entrapped in the liquid bridges, production of fines during the transportation, and lower dissolution rate [142]. Sonication is able to keep particles far apart during crystallisation, therefore reducing the formation of agglomerates and decrease the final size of the crystals.

Ultrasound has shown to promote the self-attachment of primary particles to form the final crystals (Fig. 9c). For example in the absence of ultrasound 8 h of mechanical stirring at 80 °C was required for the formation of BaTiO3 crystals, but only 20 min was required at the same temperature with a 20 kHz horn [143]. In the absence of US, crystals had irregular morphology and no aggregated structures, however with US the final crystals were aggregates of 5–10 nm small nanocrystals [144,145]. Analysis of the particles obtained with sonication revealed a single crystalline structure, i.e. the nanocrystals were oriented and attached between specific crystal faces [144]. These properties are typical of mesocrystals, aggregation of primary particles with a single-crystal structure [146]. Examples of SEM and TEM images of BaTiO3 are reported in Fig. 10 . A numerical simulation of the same system by Yasui et al. showed that only primary particles (nuclei) can aggregate with other particles and their attachment is so strong that it does not disintegrate with sonication time [147,148].

Similarly for antisolvent crystallisation of glycine, Nii et al. [83] reported the growth of the glycine crystals as a function of power at 1.6 MHz US, contrary to the size reduction observed at 20 kHz. The growth rate was modelled using the hypothesis of incorporation of microcrystals similarly to the formation of mesocrystals, and they found good agreement between the crystal sizes obtained experimentally and those calculated. This incorporation can be interpreted as crystal agglomeration, suggesting that this theory can explanation the effect behind sonocrystallisation.

A table summarising the mechanisms proposed is reported in Table 1 . Each theory presented does not necessarily exclude the others: since the phenomena behind this process are very complex, it is possible that one mechanism is not enough to explain sonocrystallisation. The theories proposed can contradict or support each other. On one hand, US seems to encourage homogeneous nucleation, while on the other hand the experiments show sonication can alter the nucleation path from homogeneous to heterogeneous because of the presence of bubbles. On the contrary, some commonalities can be drawn from deagglomeration, secondary nucleation and sonofragmentation, that is the separation of crystal particles or fragments, but they also differ in the specific mechanisms.

Therefore, it is clear why there are currently no definite theory for the observed effects from sonocrystallsation. Crystallisation process is complex and has many steps, and each step can be influenced in different ways by sonication. The parameters involved, as already explained, are many and discrepancies can arise when comparing different crystallisation and sonication studies. For example, Nii et al. [83] reported for 1.6 MHz the average crystal size increased with increasing the power, contrary to the decrease in crystal size that is often reported when more acoustic power is applied to the system [3,149–153]. Similarly, the reduction in induction period with ultrasound is the commonly reported observation, however, Kurotani et al. [84] observed an increase of the induction period if the ultrasonic energy is below a certain threshold. There needs to be a better understanding in how the sonocrystallisation mechanism could differ under different crystallisation processes (i.e. cooling, antisolvent and reactive).

Another cause of discrepancies in the literature is sonication conditions used. In order to compare different sonication studies, it is important to be able to quantify directly the cavitation activity via collapse intensity, bubble size and population, but there are currently no direct methods available. Therefore, different indirect methods such as amplifier and calorimetric power are commonly reported because they are easy to quantify, but not always an accurate indication of the cavitation activity. Sonoluminescence, sonochemilumnescence, dosimetry and acoustic pressure are better methods, but require specialised instrument. This problem is still a main issue for the acoustic cavitation community and is also affecting other fields where US is being applied.

Despite the reported discrepancies, from the reports in the last decade on frequency studies, it appears that low frequencies (<200 kHz) are better at inducing sonocrystallisation. For example, stronger reduction in the MSZW in the cooling crystallisation of paracetamol were observed at frequencies lower than 165 kHz [154], and an evident decrease of the crystal size in the anti-solvent crystallisation of sodium chloride at 44 kHz was detected respect to the other frequencies studied [155]. The reason behind this behaviour seems to be related to the transient cavitation [35,36]. In a droplet-based capillary sonocrystalliser working at 20 kHz, the amount of adipic acid crystals produced was correlated with the sonoemulsification (SE) and the sonochemiluminescence intensity (SCL) for all the supersaturation ratio investigated (S = 1.42–2.62) [36]. In addition, the sonoprobe tip was put at different distances from the capillary, between 0.7 and 10 mm. An increase in the distance showed a decrease in the amount of crystals, in the emulsification and in SCL. Since low frequency is applied, SCL is directly linked to transient cavitation bubbles, so results suggested that the transient cavitation is the responsible for the observed sonocrystallisation. Similar conclusions have been found when different frequencies (42 kHz–1.09 MHz) were applied, and at the highest frequency insufficient crystals were formed for analysis, whereas at 660 kHz the average crystal size was higher than at 42 kHz [35]. Therefore, since transient cavitation globally decreases when frequency increases, there is a strong link between crystallisation and transient cavitation.

New findings also showed that US can be applied only for a small amount of time, and not during all the crystallisation [156]. In particular, for the antisolvent crystallisation of sodium chloride, 5 s sonication applied at the beginning is enough for completing the crystallisation compared to 90 s required under silent process. This is an important result because the amount of energy required for a sonocrystallisation could be considerably decreased, making the application of US more suitable in an industrial scale.

A very insightful route toward the understanding of sonocrystallisation is the use of imaging. Since the extreme conditions initiated by cavitation and the effect on crystallisation occurs under very fast time scales, it is possible to try to image such events under high speed camera, whenever it is available. Imaging has been developing in the last decades because the technology is now mature enough for obtaining discrete results. Today, high-speed cameras are available on the market, which allow fast imaging necessary for rapid phenomena such as cavitation. In addition, new lenses have been developed for having good resolution in the microscale. Finally, the improvement of computers and memory storage have been essentials for recording and preserving videos and images with high quantity of data. Both Ohsaka and Trinh [114] and Chow [107,157] imaged the nucleation of ice by a single acoustically oscillating bubble. Both observed the formation of a single ice in dendritic form when a sufficient acoustic pressure is applied to cavitate the bubble i.e. transient bubbles. It is speculated a predendritic form of the ice nucleus is first generated at the bubble wall. As the ice crystal grows the bubble can either remain oscillating [107,157], or become unstable. In the latter case the bubble is either ejected from the initial position along with the ice nuclei, or leaves the ice nuclei behind to further grow [114]. Chow [107,157] also reported the “dancing” motion of the bubble, leaving behind the ice crystal. Similar results by Lee et al. [155], showed images of a crystallisation starting from a cluster of cavitation bubbles. The most significant is shown in Fig. 11 . During the oscillations, the cluster started moving in an irregular path, leaving behind a stream of fine sodium chloride crystals, suggesting that non-symmetrical transient cavitation is needed for inducing sonocrystallisation.

Many sonocrystallisation studies have been reported since the publication of the first literature review on sonocrystallisation by Hem in 1967 [85]. Thanks to the huge improvement in the technology since the 1980s, both sonication equipment and instruments for analysis have progressed to increase the efficiency and accuracy of US systems and measurements. Since that first review, the main novelties are the introduction of modelling and image capturing as essential tools to combine with the classical experiments. However, complete knowledge of the mechanism remains unknown and, therefore prevents the optimum use of US for sonocrystallisation, in terms of reactor design, energy consumption and product quality. Finding the mechanism governing sonocrystallisation seems to become more and more important, since the researchers working in the topic are aware of the potential behind sonication.

The scientific community working on sonocrystallisation is increasing and additional work will contribute toward an improved understanding of the phenomena. It is however important to be cautious when interpreting and comparing results obtained from different systems, since possible contradictions found could be related to small differences in the equipment and crystallisation systems used.

More systematic studies are needed in order to have a clearer view of the phenomena involved. This means choosing a fixed system for crystallisation, using the same equipment (amplifier, transducers) and varying systematically frequency, power and sonication time. In addition, cavitation activity should be quantified using the techniques available (dosimetry, sonoluminescence) to completely characterise the system. Modelling and imaging, are powerful tools to compliment experimental data. However, current modelling is still limited for computing crystallisation and predicting multi-bubbles systems, and high speed imaging requires sophisticated equipment. A multidisciplinary approach could be the solution to such complex problem as sonocrystallisation, but a good combination of proper equipment and fundamental knowledge are essential for designing experiments to unravel the mystery behind sonocrystallisation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
